coredata.dc:title: Exploration of highly photoluminescent first-row transition metals (manganese, iron, cobalt, nickel, copper and zinc) co-doped nano carbon dots as energy storage materials
coredata.dc:creator: Daniel, Sobhi
Praveena, M.G.
Mohammed, E.M.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots have currently emerged as a unique and promising material focusing on advanced applications especially in the field of optoelectronic and energy storage devices. The preferential doping of nanocarbon dots with first row transition elements will enhance the photoluminescence characteristics and thereby, we can fine-tune its photophysical properties. In the present work, we have synthesized a series of first-row transition metal atoms (Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+) co-doped with nitrogen and nitrogen- sulfur carbon dots by a greener synthetic strategy. The formation of doped carbon dots were confirmed by its characteristic surface plasmon resonance absorptions using the Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy. The presence of major elements and the functional group identification was done with the aid of EDS and FT-IR spectral analysis, the morphology and size were analyzed by high-resolution TEM analysis and photophysical characteristics were investigated from the fluorescence measurements. The photoluminescence studies reveal that by co-doping the transition metals with nitrogen or nitrogen- sulfur carbon dots, the emissions maxima of the nanodots can be tuned. The doped carbon dots were explored for their energy storage application and their super capacitance characteristics. The capacitance values of the carbon dots were found to be increased with the effect of co-doping with the first-row transition elements and the capacitance values were in the range of 2.6 µF/cm2 to 0.5 µF/cm2 and are in tune with the capacitance values for carbon-based materials including graphene and C-dots reported in the literature. Also the cyclic voltammetric loops were found to be sufficiently rectangular in shape, desirable for a supercapacitor, and the rectangular areas were found to be increased with increase in scan rate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Doping
Photoluminescence
Energy storage
Supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur doped C-dots
Synthesis of transition metal ion doped C-dots (TMCDs)
Capacitance and dielectric loss factor measurements

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis of transition metal atom doped carbon dots.

(a): Visual appearance of carbon dots under visible light and UV light (a) N doped (b) Mn2+ doped (c) Fe2+ doped (d) Co2+ doped (e) Ni2+ doped (f) Cu2+ doped (g) Zn2+ doped. (b) Visual appearance of carbon dots under visible light and UV light (a) N,S doped (b) Mn2+ doped (c) Fe2+ doped (d) Co2+ doped (e) Ni2+ doped (f) Cu2+ doped (g) Zn2+ doped.
(a) UV–Visible Spectra of N doped C-dots and transition metal atom co-doped carbon dots (b) UV–Visible Spectra of N, S doped C-dots and transition metal atom co-doped carbon dots.
The IR spectrum of N, Mn- co doped Carbon dot.
(a) High resolution TEM images of N doped and N-co-doped transition metal carbon dots. (b): High resolution TEM images of N,S co-doped transition metal carbon dots.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of N -co-doped transition metal–carbon dots (b) N, S co-doped transition metal–carbon dots.
Experimental set up for capacitance measurements.

(a): Variation of capacitance with respect frequency (a) transition metals co-doped N carbon dots (b) transition metals co-doped N,S carbon dots (b): Variation of dielectric loss factor (D) with respect to frequency(a) transition metals co-doped N carbon dots (b) transition metals co-doped N,S carbon dots.
(a) The Cyclic Voltamograms of N doped and transition metal co doped carbon dots (b) The Cyclic Voltamograms of N,S doped and transition metal co doped carbon dots.
IR spectral characteristics of N doped and transition metal co-doped N carbon dots.
IR spectral characteristics of N,S doped and transition metal co-doped N,S carbon dots.
Energy dispersive spectra (EDS) analysis of N doped and transition metal co doped carbon dots.
Energy dispersive spectra (EDS) analysis of N, S doped and transition metal co doped carbon dots.
Comparison of the capacitance values of the synthesized carbon dots.
Comaparison of capacitances values of the TMCDs with the existing literature repots.

head.ce:title: Exploration of highly photoluminescent first-row transition metals (manganese, iron, cobalt, nickel, copper and zinc) co-doped nano carbon dots as energy storage materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Maharaja’s Technological Institute, Thrissur, Kerala 20, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Maharaja’s Technological Institute, Thrissur, Kerala 20, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Postgraduate and Research Department of Chemistry, Maharaja’s College, Ernakulam, Kochi, Kerala 682011, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Postgraduate and Research Department of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Postgraduate and Research Department of Chemistry, Maharaja’s College, Ernakulam, Kochi, Kerala 682011, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Postgraduate and Research Department of Physics, Maharaja’s College, Ernakulam, Kochi, Kerala 682011, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Postgraduate and Research Department of Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Postgraduate and Research Department of Physics, Maharaja’s College, Ernakulam, Kochi, Kerala 682011, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Maharaja’s Technological Institute, Thrissur, Kerala 20, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots have currently emerged as a unique and promising material focusing on advanced applications especially in the field of optoelectronic and energy storage devices. The preferential doping of nanocarbon dots with first row transition elements will enhance the photoluminescence characteristics and thereby, we can fine-tune its photophysical properties. In the present work, we have synthesized a series of first-row transition metal atoms (Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+) co-doped with nitrogen and nitrogen- sulfur carbon dots by a greener synthetic strategy. The formation of doped carbon dots were confirmed by its characteristic surface plasmon resonance absorptions using the Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy. The presence of major elements and the functional group identification was done with the aid of EDS and FT-IR spectral analysis, the morphology and size were analyzed by high-resolution TEM analysis and photophysical characteristics were investigated from the fluorescence measurements. The photoluminescence studies reveal that by co-doping the transition metals with nitrogen or nitrogen- sulfur carbon dots, the emissions maxima of the nanodots can be tuned. The doped carbon dots were explored for their energy storage application and their super capacitance characteristics. The capacitance values of the carbon dots were found to be increased with the effect of co-doping with the first-row transition elements and the capacitance values were in the range of 2.6 µF/cm2 to 0.5 µF/cm2 and are in tune with the capacitance values for carbon-based materials including graphene and C-dots reported in the literature. Also the cyclic voltammetric loops were found to be sufficiently rectangular in shape, desirable for a supercapacitor, and the rectangular areas were found to be increased with increase in scan rate.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots can be portrayed as a magnificent and supreme material among other nanomaterials of the current century. The sp2 hybridized carbon core and its excellent fluorescence properties launched a new horizon in its advanced applications fields such as sensing, bio imaging., targeted drug delivery, fabrication of composite materials, etc. [1–5]. The most significant inherent property of biocompatibility and biodegradability brand the carbon dots a viable tool towards the applications focusing on biological systems in comparison to the conventional quantum dots [6–8]. Even though numerous synthetic strategies were reported in the literature regarding the synthesis of carbon dots, the green synthetic methods espoused with precursors that are greener in its origin and microwave-assisted pyrolysis gained significant attention because of the maximum yield, lower reaction time, efficiency, etc [9–11].

One of the prominent pathways for upgrading the inherent properties of carbon dots is by doping the carbon dots with other heteroatoms [12–14]. Nitrogen and sulfur atoms were found to be excellent dopants because of their comparable size and properties in comparison to that of carbon dots as reported in the literature. Generally, doping and/or co-doping are mainly performed to control the photoluminescence spectacle for enlightening their fluorescence efficiency. The carbon-based nanomaterials are popular for their mesmerizing optical and electronic properties and their potential applications in energy conversion and storage [15]. Contrasting to traditional energy materials, carbon nanomaterials have some unusual size-/surface-dependent (e.g., morphological, electrical, optical, and mechanical) properties, which are extremely beneficial in enhancing energy- conversion and storage performance [16]. Because of their ultrafine particle sizes and exceptional physicochemical properties, nano carbon dots have attracted significant attention in formulating high-performance energy storage materials in the recent past [17–21]. Also, carbon dots were found to play imperative roles in refining the conductivity and electrochemical kinetics of other active material.

The existing literature reports reveal the use of non-metals such as boron, fluorine, and phosphorus in addition to the nitrogen and sulphur doped and co-doped carbon dots [22–24]. Takashi Nozaki et al. reported nitrogen-doped C-dots (N-CDs) using tannic acid and polyethyleneimine as precursors and the nanocomposite of N-CDs with Au NPs (N-CD–Au NP nanocomposite) [25]. Zinc-doped carbon dots were synthesized by Quan Xu et al. and they have determined the effect of the size of the carbon dots and how the doping amount affects the photoluminescent quantum yield of carbon dots [26]. Sajid Abdul Rub Pakkath et al. reported the synthesis of transition metal ion namely Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, and Ni2+ doped carbon dots, and explored its application as a potential nanoprobe for magneto-fluorescent dual-modality bioimaging studies [27]. Blue fluorescent and photostable Mn2+ [28–30] doped carbon dots also reported in the literature. The magnified luminescence quenching impact upon binding of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots to transition metal ions was recently reported by Anjali Devi et al. [31].

Electrochemical supercapacitors have engrossed many researchers in recent years because of their high-power density than batteries, long cyclic life, and higher energy density than conventional dielectric capacitors as energy-storage devices. In addition to that supercapacitors groove high in terms of environmental friendliness amid the energy-storage devices. Srikant Sahoo et al. [32] have been recently synthesized C-dots from lemon juice by adapting the hydrothermal route to prepare NiS incorporated with C-dots nanocomposite. Embedded capacitors will substitute current distinct capacitors and can be expansively used in next-generation contemporary electronics. Both high dielectric constant and low dielectric loss in dielectrics are highly desired in embedded capacitor applications. Tian Chen et al. [33] concocted a graphene quantum dot-cobalt ferrite-poly(vinylidene fluoride) ternary composite with high dielectric constant and low dielectric loss. Yan Qing et al. [34] developed a unique strategy to construct an overall conductive network within the activated carbon by embedding highly crystallized graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The capacitive and rate concerts of the GQD embedded activated carbons astonishingly increased along with the enhanced addition ratio of GQD. CDs were successfully anchored on GO by Yong - Qiang Dang et al. and their studies revealed the electrochemical performances of the CDs/ reduced graphene oxide (rGO) as an active supercapacitor [35]. A novel hybrid supercapacitor was constructed using α-MnO2 nanorods decorated with C-Dots and rGO as electrode material along with a special type of ionic liquid membrane as an electrolyte and its electrical properties were characterized by Rukan Gen et al. [36]. Dharmalingam et al. [37] investigated the electrochemical performance ofnitrogen andoxygen co-doped carbon dots derived fromagreen precursor forsupercapacitor applications. Phosphorus and nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots were synthesized by a solvothermal method, the electrochemical studies of nanodots were analyzed effectively and used them as a fluorescent probe for iron(III) by Khalid M. Omer et al. [38]. Recently Yan et al. [39] reported the synthesis of core shell heirarchichal porous carbon nano spheres with N/O doping based on Schiff’s chemistry and investigated their energy storage capacities. The development of heteroatom-doped hydrangea-like porous carbons with ion-comparable pore sizes for advanced carbon based super capacitors using gel-polymer supported electrolytes were reported by Song et al. [40]. Gu et al. [41] synthesized a N/O doped new nonporous metal–organic framework (NPMOF) using rigid nitrogen-containing ligand based on carbonization reaction and evaluated their supercapacitor performances. The recent advances in the development of carbon-based electrodes and electrolytes for super capacitor applications are elaborately reviewed by Miao et al. [42].

Even though enormous literature reports are available regarding the synthesis of heteroatom doped carbon dots and the energy storage applications of carbon dots, there is any comparative studies reported regarding the first-row transition elements co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots focusing their energy storage application and their supercapacitor performances. In this context, herein we report the green synthesis, characterization, electronic and fluorescent characteristics and the efficiency of transition metal ions doped (Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+) nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots focusing its energy storage applications.

The synthesis of carbon dots was carried out using an LG domestic programmable microwave oven. The UV–visible spectra were recorded on a UV-1800 Shimadzu, Japan, Spectrometer by dissolving the C-dots in distilled water. The high-resolution TEM studies were done with the help of HR-TEM, JEOL JEM 2100, Japan. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Perkin Elmer FT-IR spectrometer, USA. The elemental analysis was carried out using an EDS analyzer, Carl-Zeiss Supra-55 analyzer, Germany. The fluorescence spectra were collected using a FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon GmbH, München, Germany). The dielectric measurements were done using an impedance analyser (WAYNE KERR 6500B) in the frequency range 100 Hz to 10 MHz at room temperature. The cyclic voltammetric analysis was carried out in a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (CH instruments, USA) with a three electrode system.

Fresh lemons (Citrus lemon) were secured from a local vegetable market. Ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (sodium salt) (EDTA), manganese sulphate (MnSO4·H2O), ferrous sulphate, (FeSO4.7 H2O), Cobalt sulphate (CoSO4·7H2O), nickel sulphate (NiSO4·6H2O), Copper sulphate (CuSO4. 5 H2O) and zinc sulphate (ZnSO4·7H2O)) were purchased from Merck. All the materials obtained were used without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mM (1.2 mg) urea/thiourea (1.52 mg) and 0.05 mM EDTA (0.33 mg) were weighed accurately and transferred to a mortar and ground it properly, dissolved the mixture in 10 ml of distilled water and quantitatively transferred to a 100 ml beaker and 10 ml of filtered fresh lemon juice was added to the above mixture and is stirred well. The lemon extract - urea/ thiourea- EDTA mixture was then irradiated in a microwave oven at 500 W for 10 min. The colour of the solution turned from light yellow to dark brown scales, indicating the formation of EDTA- functionalized C-dots. The dark brown coloured carbon dots obtained were again dissolved in 10 ml of distilled water, centrifuged three times, the mixture at 10000 rpm discarded the precipitate obtained and the supernatant (N and S doped carbon dots) was collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of transition metal ion doped C-dots (TMCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 mM (0.60 mg) urea / thiourea (0.76 mg), 0.5 mM transition metal ion precursors, 1.64 mg manganese sulphate (MnSO4·H2O), 2.78 mg ferrous sulphate, (FeSO4.7 H2O), 2.81 mg Cobalt sulphate (CoSO4·7H2O), 2.62 mg nickel sulphate (NiSO4·6H2O), 2.49 mg Copper sulphate (CuSO4. 5 H2O) or 2.87 mg zinc sulphate (ZnSO4·7H2O) and 0.05 mM (0.33 mg) EDTA were mixed thoroughly in a mortar, dissolved the mixture in 10 ml of distilled water and quantitatively transferred to a 100 ml beaker. 10 ml of lemon juice was added to the above mixture, mixed properly and the solution mixture was irradiated in a microwave oven at 500 W for 10 min. The solution colour changed from turbid light yellow to deep brown scales, indicating the formation of transition metal atom doped carbon dots. The dark brown coloured carbon dots obtained were again dissolved in 10 ml of distilled water. Centrifuged the mixture three times at 10000 rpm. Discarded the precipitate obtained and the supernatant (transition metal atom doped carbon dots) was collected.

Dielectric characterization of the samples was done using an impedance analyser (WAYNE KERR 6500B) in the frequency range 100 Hz to 10 MHz at room temperature. Silver coating was given on the top and bottom surfaces of cylindrical pellets of the ferrite samples to make a parallel plate capacitor geometry with the ferrite material as the dielectric medium between the plates. The capacitance of such a parallel plate capacitor is given by
C =
∊ 0
∊
r
A
d
where A and d are the area and thickness of the pellet respectively, 0 is the permittivity of free space, and r is the dielectric permittivity of the given sample. The dielectric permittivity of the samples has been calculated using the relation,
∊ r
=
Cd
∊ r
A

The capacitance values were calculated from the impedance analysis and the carbon dots itself were served as the electrolytes. The synthesized transition metal co-doped nano carbon dots (liquid samples) were taken in a Teflon container of 10 mm diameter and 4 mm thickness. Copper plates were used as electrodes. Capacitance (C) and dielectric loss (D) were measured as a function of frequency in the range of 100 Hz − 10 MHz using an impedance analyser (Wayne Kerr H-6500B model.

The redox characteristics of N doped N,S doped and transition metal codoped carbon dots were examined by cyclic voltammetry using conventional three electrode system. Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode and platinum wire as the counter electrode. Glassy carbon (GC, 3 mm in diameter, CH Instruments, USA) modified with carbon dots were used as a working electrode. 0.1 M KCl was used as a supporting electrolyte. For the preparation of the working electrode, the GC electrode was sequentially polished with slurries of 0.5 and 0.3 μm alumina to create a mirror finish. It was then sonicated for 5 min in ethanol and then washed with double-distilled water to remove traces of alumina grit from its surface. Then the electrode was rinsed thoroughly with double-distilled water and dried at ambient temperature, 100 μL of the carbon dots were mixed with 50 μL Nafion (0.05%) and the mixture mixed thoroughly for 1 min in a vortex. Then the mixture was casted on to the surface of the electrode with a microsyringe, and was allowed to evaporate in the air at ambient temperature.

In the present investigation, we have synthesized a series of transition metal co-doped N and S carbon dots by a single pot synthetic strategy by utilizing the building blocks as urea/thiourea, EDTA, different transition metal salts viz Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+ and lemon juice (Citrus lemon). The transition metal co-doped carbon dots having photoluminescence characteristics were explored for their energy storage applications and their supercapacitor performances.

The phases tangled during the amalgamation of transition metal (Mn2+, Fe 2+, Co 2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+) co-doped N and S C-dots using urea/thiourea, EDTA and lemon juice is based on the pyrolytic carbonization of the precursors by the irradiation of microwaves. The microwave -supported pyrolysis was carried out at 500 W for 10 min under ambient conditions, favoured the formation of corresponding metal oxide traces on the surface of N and Sulphur based carbon dots.

This method is elegant, green, simple, and environment-friendly, with minimum cost. The synthetic strategy adopted for the synthesis of transition metal co-doped N and S carbon dots under microwave irradiation is schematically shown in Fig. 1 .

The visual appearance of the transition metal co-doped nitrogen and sulfur carbon dots derived from urea/thiourea, EDTA, and lemon juice were shown in Fig. 2 (a & b). The carbon dots formed are yellow to brown in visual appearance and all the transition metal co-doped carbon dots exhibited green coloration with blue fluorescence in the upper layer when placed under a UV lamp.

The UV–visible absorption spectra will give insight and prolific information regarding the surface plasmon resonance absorption bands corresponding to nano-sized materials. The absorption spectra of N and S doped carbon dots and transition metal incorporated carbon dots (CDs) were shown in Fig. 3 . The localized resonance absorption of N, S carbon dots and co-doped transition metal-doped carbon dots exhibits, a broad characteristic peak observed at 345 ± 3 nm due to n − π* transition originated from the C = O groups and also due to the contribution of other surface enveloped functional groups. A dip was also observed at 280 ± 5 nm due to π − π* electronic transition due to the surface-bending characteristic of graphitic sp2-carbon on C-dot surfaces. It can be noticed that the absorption maximum of the N dot carbon dots co-doped with Mn and Ni atom showed a redshift in the absorption spectrum while the Fe, Co, Cu, and Zn co-doped carbon dots showed a slight blue shift in the UV visible spectrum. In the case of N, S co-doped transition metals also the absorption maximum is centred around 345nnm while the absorption maximum gets slightly red-shifted in the case of zinc as co dopants and gets blue-shifted in the case of manganese co-doped carbon dots.

The existence of main functional groups presents in different sets of transition metal co-doped N carbon dots and N, S carbon dots were further confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy. The major frequency peaks observed were summarised in Tables 1 and 2
. The characteristic frequencies observed in the IR spectra were corresponding to CO, CC, CO, CH, NH, OH, and MO bonds. The broad peak centered around 3400 cm−1 present in each set of carbon dots can be attributed due to the –OH stretching vibrations, indicating the existence of hydroxyl groups, which facilitate the excellent solubility of carbon dots in water and enduring its stability. The broad absorption band presents near 2080 cm−1 indicates the presence of CH bonds. Additional carboxyl groups were confirmed by the existence of sharp peaks near 1700 cm−1, credited to CO bonds and the peak observed near 1400 cm−1 can be attributed to NH stretching vibrations, In the case of transition metal atoms co-doped with N and N, S carbon dots an additional peak corresponding to MO bond appeared and the frequencies occurred were in the range of 400–615 cm−1 in different metal-doped carbon dots, which strongly supports the incorporation of metal oxides on the surface of carbon dots. In the case of N, S carbon dots and transition metal codoped carbon dots a very weak peak occurs at a wavelength of 2445 cm1 and can be attributed to the -S-H stectching frequency. The IR spectral peaks observed in Mn co-doped N carbon dot is shown in Fig. 4 as a representative of the transition metal co-doped carbon dots.

The IR spectra of different carbon dots thus clearly reveal that carbon dots were functionalized with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid groups, and the transition metal atoms are excellently incorporated in the carbonaceous core structures.

The surface composition of the N doped, N, S doped and the transition metal co doped carbon dots were estimated using an EDS analyzer. The percentage of C, O, N, S and the various transition elements obtained were shown in Tables 3 and 4
. The EDS analysis clearly indicates the co doping of transition elements during the synthesis of carbon dots.

The size and morphology of the doped carbon dots were examined using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The HRTEM images of N co-doped transition metal–carbon dots and N, S co-doped transition metal–carbon dots were shown in Fig. 5 (a & b) respectively. The TEM images reveal that the particles are homogeneously distributed in the case of N doped and N, S doped carbon dots, and are almost found to be hexagonal shaped having a size range of less than 20 nm. After co-doping with transition metal atoms, the crystallinity of the carbon dots gets enhanced and the transition metal atoms are packed hexagonally over the surface of the carbon dots but not in a homogeneous fashion. Also, the particle sizes were found to be comparatively higher in transition metal co-doped carbon dots compared to N doped or N, S doped carbon dots. Also, the size of the carbon dots was different in each co-doped transition metal atoms and the images of the N-Mn and N-Co co-doped carbon dot was found to be less than 10 nm in size and was found to be almost uniformly distributed in the carbon core.

The fluorescence measurements of N doped carbon dots, N- co-doped transition metal atoms, N, S doped carbon dots, and N, S-co-doped transition metal atoms were carried out at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The fluorescence spectra recorded for N co-doped transition metal–carbon dots and N, S co-doped transition metal–carbon dots were shown in Fig. 6 (a & b) respectively. Carbon dots exhibited a strong blue photoluminescence response, with a peak centered at 450 nm in the case of N doped carbon dots and 443 nm in the case of N, S doped carbon dots. The inherent photoluminescent characteristics of nano carbon dots were found to be modified in the presence of first row transition elements and it is interesting to notice that by doping with sulfur, the emission wavelength got blue shifted. In the case of transition metal co-doped N carbon dots, the emission wavelength occurs at same wavelength in the case of Mn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+ ions while in the case of Fe2+ the emission wavelength got red-shifted to 458 nm while in the case of Co2+ it is blue-shifted to 443 nm.

The fluorescence spectra of N, S co-doped transition metal–carbon dots show more fine-tuned fluorescence patterns compared to the N co-doped transition metal–carbon dots. In the case of Mn2+ and Fe2+, the emission maxima were red-shifted to 445 nm and 447 nm respectively. While in the case of Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+, the emission maxima got blue-shifted to 432, 442, 433, and 438 nm respectively. Thus, the surface states of the carbon dots will play a prominent impact on its photophysical properties. The strong photoluminescent emission of the transition metal co-doped N and N, S carbon dots may mainly result from the surface-doped transition metals ions along with nitrogen and/or sulfur atoms present in the carbon dots. From these measurements, we can notice that by selectively doping the carbon dots with other dopants such as transition metal atoms, we can fine-tune the emission spectra of carbon dots and will find wide application in the fabrication of novel optoelectronic materials in near future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capacitance and dielectric loss factor measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors (SCs) are one of the potential and feasible energy storage devices having high power density and fast charge–discharge capacity, which rendered them as highly prospective and most challenging energy storage devices. Although from an architecture point of view, these are very much similar to solid electrode batteries, it has thousands of times high power density, low maintenance, and long-life cycle.

The global presentation of a SC is mainly administered by the type of electrodes, separator, current collector, and electrolytes. SC stores charge in the form of capacitance and can be calculated by using Eq. (1)
(1)
C = i / m
dV / d t
.
where i is the current in amperes, m is either the mass, volume, or area.
dV/dt is the change in voltage over the change in time in volts per second.

Power density and maximum energy can be calculated according to the Eqs. (2) and (3).
(2)
Energy
d e n s i t y =
1 2
c
V 2
(3)
Maximum
P o w e r =
V i 2
4 m
R s
Is the initial voltage of the device in volts, Rs is the equivalent series resistance in ohms, and V is the voltage of the SC in volts.

The experimental set up adopted for the measurement of capacitance is schematically represented in Fig. 7 . The Capacitance initially decreases abruptly with an increase in frequency. However, it remains almost constant at higher frequencies for all samples (Fig. 7(a)).

From our data, it is clear that the capacitance values get increased with the effect of co-doping with the first-row transition elements in the case of N doped and N, S doped carbon dots and the capacitance values lie in the range of 2.6 µF/cm2 to 0.5 µF/cm2. The Capacitance values reported in the literature for carbon-based materials including graphene and C-dots vary between 0.08 and 50 µF/cm2 [36], which is within the range that was obtained in the present study.

Also, it is interestingly noticed that the co-dopants such as Mn2+, Fe2+, and Co2+ are promising candidates for enhancing the super capacitance performance of the nanocarbon dots. The capacitance measurements were found to be almost fivefold enhanced by the addition of co-dopants as transition metals along with the N doped and N, S doped carbon dots. The capacitance values obtained for N doped, N, S doped, and transition metal co-doped carbon dots are shown in Table 5 .

Even though the capacitance values obtained in the cased of co-doped transition metals do not show a general trend, the high capacitance values were observed in the case of Mn2+, Fe2+ or Co2+ and can be attributed to the presence of more number of unpaired electrons present in these metal species in comparison to other transition metal ions. The capacitance enhancement is possibly due to the introduction of the high surface area of the carbon dots, the presence of different active functional groups in the surface sites, and the incorporation of transition metal atoms in the carbogenic core of the carbon dots.
A comparative study of the capacitance values of the TMCDs carbon dots with the the existing literature reported is shown in Table 6

Nano carbon dots can be considered as a pertinent precursor material for the creation of supercapacitors owing to their sufficient carbonisation structures. The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in carbon dots brands them to display a strong hydrophilic nature as an electrode material, which is beneficial for taming the wettability of the electrolyte and thereby improving the interfacial compatibility. The process of heteroatom doping is highly favorable to the growth of electron-rich regions on the electrode surfaces. The comparatively inferior life cycle of the transition metal oxide electrode materials inhibits their application in electrochemical energy storage and in this context the hybrid combination of nano carbon dots integrated with transition metal atoms will be an elegant solution in the fabtication of greener electrode materials. Also, the fascinating nature of transition metal codoped carbon dots for the energy related application can be credited to the astonishing optoelectronic properties and their higher electrical conductivity.

Fig. 8 (b) represents the variation of dielectric loss factor concerning frequency. In the case of supercapacitors, the dielectric losses within the capacitor start to occur only when the frequencies are very much higher compared to the frequencies adopted for the capacitance measurements. From the figure, we can notice that the dielectric loss occurs only at a frequency greater than 10000 Hz and confirms the effectiveness of transition metal co-doped carbon dot in super capacitance applications. Thus by effecting co-doping, the dielectric constant values can be improved with a low dielectric loss factor. The efficiency of the capacitors can be enhanced by selective doping or mixed doping of the transition elements and by measuring the capacitance studies to a still lower frequency range. These studies are in progress and we hope the co-doped transition element nanocarbon dots will be a promising candidate for the energy storage and super capacitance applications.

Cyclic voltammetric analysis will provide a quantitative and qualitative data relating to the electrochemical phenomena taking place in the active materials of the working electrode. The N doped, N, S doped and the co doped carbon dots coated on the glassy carbon electrode will act as the working electrode in the present investigation and will give an insight in to the electrochemical reactions ie, oxidation and reduction process occurring in the electrode with respect to the change in current and voltage. From the capacitance measurements, the transition meatals Mn2+, Fe 2+ and Co2+ co doped with both nitrogen and nitrogen, sulfur carbon nanodots offered better performances and so we have restricted the voltammetric analysis to these samples alone. The CV studies were carried out in the potential range of − 0.8 to 1 V in 0.1 M KCl as the electrolyte at various scan rates (30, 50, 80, 100 and 200 mV/s). The CV curves exhibit a rectangular shaped curve in transition metals co doped with N and N, S carbon dots in higher scan rates. The voltamograms obtained were as shown in Fig. 9 (a & b). The rectangular shaped curves revealed the electrical double layer capacitor (EDLC) with pseudocapacitance behaviour. Qualitatively, the CV loop was found to be sufficiently rectangular in shape, which is desirable for a supercapacitor, and the rectangular areas were found to be increased with increased scan rate, as expected.

The effect of doping may be one of the reasons for the enhanced specific capacitance. The inherent properties of nano carbon dots such as ultra-fineness, edges, moieties, high electrical conductivity, and ion donating ability integrated with transition elements will definitely emerge as a novel class of green electrode materials, which can be effectively utilized in the energy storage applications and supercapacitors in near future.

Highly luminescent first-row transition elements (Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+) co-doped with nitrogen and nitrogen-sulfur carbon dots were synthesized by a greener microwave-assisted pyrolysis pathway and the optical and physicochemical properties of these doped carbon dots were analysed using various analytical methods. The introduction of transition metal atoms in these carbon nanodots leads to a remarkable fluorescence enhancement and thereby we can fine-tune the emission properties of nanocarbon dots. The above phenomenon can be attributed to the surface plasmon effect and the incorporation of transition metal atoms as its oxide on the surface of the nitrogen or nitrogen-sulfur carbon nanodots. The synthesized transition metal co-doped carbon dots emitted bright blue fluorescence and the emission maxima were slightly got shifted to a longer or shorter wavelength in some specific transition metal toms and hence, photo-emissive properties of the doped and co-doped carbon dots can be fine-tuned by the preferential/ selective doping of heteroatoms. The highly fluorescent transition metal nanocarbon dots exhibited significant super capacitance properties and will be a promising material for its energy storage. The capacitance values were found to be increased with the effect of co-doping with the first-row transition elements in the case of N doped and N, S doped carbon dots and the capacitance values lie in the range of 2.6 µF/cm2 to 0.5 µF/cm2 and the values were in accordance with the capacitance values reported in the literature for carbon-based materials including graphene and C-dots.). The rectangular shaped cyclic voltammograms predicts the electrical double layer capacitor (EDLC) with pseudocapacitance behaviour for the co doped carbon dots. Also the CV loops were found to be sufficiently rectangular in shape, which is desirable for a supercapacitor, and the rectangular areas were found to be increased with increased scan rate, as expected. The good processibility and flexibility of nanocarbon dots represent the advantage of their effective application in energy storage devices. Also, the exploitation of these transition metal atom doped and co-doped carbon dots in a wide variety of applications including the biological and superparamagnetic applications are in progress.

The synthesis and characterization studies of transition metal carbon dots were carried out by Dr. Sobhi Daniel, the cyclic voltametric analysis were done by Praveena M.G. The manuscript prepation was done by Dr. Sobhi Daniel and Prof. E.M. Mohammed.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors acknowledge CUSAT for HR-TEM , EDS analysis and CV measurements and UC college, Aluva for the fluorescence measurements.
The authors confirm that the data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article [and/or] its supplementary material.
